newHtml:
The "stoning verse" — Umar says it was in the Quran; it no longer is Women Logical Inconsistency Strong Bukhari 4350
Umar: "Allah sent Muhammad with the Truth and revealed the Holy Book to him, and among what Allah revealed was the Verse of Ar-Rajm (stoning to death)... Allah's Apostle carried out stoning, and so did we after him. I am afraid that after a long time has passed, somebody will say, 'By Allah, we do not find the Verse of Ar-Rajm in Allah's Book.'"

What the hadith says

Umar — the second caliph — explicitly states the Quran once contained a "verse of stoning" (ayat al-rajm) commanding death for adultery. He recited it: "When a man and woman commit adultery, stone them outright as a punishment from Allah." This verse does not appear in any current Quran.

Why this is a problem

The Quran claims perfect preservation (Q 15:9, Q 85:21–22). Umar — widely considered the most reliable companion after Abu Bakr — explicitly says a revealed verse is missing from the current text. This creates an iron trilemma: (1) Umar was wrong — but he was a trusted memorizer and his testimony is in Sahih collections; (2) the verse was real but lost — contradicting Q 15:9; (3) the verse was "abrogated in recitation but not in ruling" — a category that allows divine text to be removed from the book while remaining legally binding, a category the Quran itself does not describe.

Why it fails

The naskh al-tilawa doctrine introduces a category of divine command that is operative but absent from the book Allah promised to preserve. If divine commands can be binding while absent from the Quran, the book's completeness as a legal source is broken. The preservation claim — "We will be its guardian" (Q 15:9) — loses meaning if the book contains only the commands Allah decided to leave in, not all commands he gave.

Women's "deficient intelligence" proved by a witness rule that itself rests on their deficient intelligence Women Logical Inconsistency Strong Bukhari 301
"Muhammad said: 'Is not the evidence of two women equal to the witness of one man?' They replied in the affirmative. He said, 'This is the deficiency in her intelligence. Isn't it true that a woman can neither pray nor fast during her menses?' The women replied in the affirmative. He said, 'This is the deficiency in her religion.'"

What the hadith says

When women ask Muhammad to explain "deficient in intelligence and religion," he gives two proofs: (1) the Quran's two-female-equals-one-male witness rule proves intellectual deficiency; (2) women's exemption from prayer during menstruation proves religious deficiency.

Why this is a problem

The reasoning is circular. The Quran's witness rule is cited as evidence of women's intellectual deficiency — but the rule was presumably established because of some presumed deficiency. The rule assumes the conclusion it is used to prove. The second argument is worse: Islamic law exempts women from prayer during menstruation as an accommodation. The hadith then declares women religiously deficient because they do not pray during menstruation — condemning them for obeying a command they were given.

Why it fails

Classical Islamic jurisprudence operationalized this hadith as normative: women cannot be judges (citing intellectual deficiency), cannot lead prayers for men (citing religious deficiency), and women's testimony carries half the legal weight. The "merely descriptive" reading contradicts 1,400 years of jurisprudence that treated Muhammad's statement as substantive justification for legal inequality. The circular logic was not noticed or corrected by classical scholars — it was embedded in the architecture of Islamic law.

Muhammad predicts no one alive tonight will be alive in 100 years — part of a failed end-times pattern Contradiction Strong Bukhari 116
"Once the Prophet led us in the 'Isha' prayer during the last days of his life and after finishing it he said: 'Do you realize (the importance of) this night? Nobody present on the surface of the earth tonight will be living after the completion of one hundred years from this night.'"

What the hadith says

In the last years of his life (c. 632 CE), Muhammad made a specific falsifiable prediction: no one alive on earth at that moment would be alive 100 years later.

Why this is a problem

Classical commentators struggled with this precisely because they understood Muhammad was making a statement of eschatological urgency — implying the end times were near. This links to a broader pattern of hadith predicting imminent apocalypse: "the Hour and I are as these two" (Muhammad holding up two fingers); "the Last Hour will come before a generation passes." Muhammad appears to have expected the end much sooner than it came. When it did not arrive, the tradition retroactively reinterpreted the predictions as metaphorical or as referring to different events — a standard pattern for founding figures with imminent-end expectations.

Why it fails

The "generational transition" reading requires the statement to mean something other than what it says — "nobody present on the surface of the earth" is not limited to Muhammad's companions. The broader pattern of urgency-predictions in the hadith corpus confirms what the plain text suggests: Muhammad expected the end much sooner than it came, and the tradition has retroactively reinterpreted failed predictions just as other religious traditions have done.

Muhammad orders the assassination of Ka'b bin al-Ashraf — a poet who criticized him Prophetic Character Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Bukhari 2907
"The Prophet said, 'Who is ready to kill Ka'b bin Al-Ashraf who has really hurt Allah and His Apostle?' Muhammad bin Maslama said, 'O Allah's Apostle! Do you like me to kill him?' He replied in the affirmative... Muhammad bin Maslama said, 'Then allow me to say what I like.' The Prophet replied, 'I do (i.e. allow you).'"

What the hadith says

Ka'b bin al-Ashraf was a Jewish poet who wrote verses criticizing Muhammad after the Battle of Badr. Muhammad asked for volunteers to kill him, granted permission to lie to lure Ka'b out, and the team killed him by deception.

Why this is a problem

  1. The offense was literary criticism. Ka'b's crime was writing poetry critical of Muhammad — "hurting Allah and His Apostle" is the language of blasphemy, not armed threat. Muhammad ordered assassination for a poet's words.
  2. Muhammad explicitly authorized deception to accomplish the killing. "Say what you like" is permission to lie for the purpose of murder. This is preserved as prophetic precedent — deception in service of eliminating a critic is authorized by the Prophet himself.

Why it fails

The hadith identifies the offense as "hurting Allah and His Apostle" — blasphemy language, not military threat. Ka'b was a member of the Banu Nadir, which had a non-aggression arrangement with Medina at the time. Modern defenders of blasphemy killings cite this precedent exactly because it represents prophetic authorization of killing critics — not because they are misreading it.

An angel writes your entire life story — deeds, death date, and paradise or hell — before you are born Logical Inconsistency Strong Bukhari 3075
"Allah sends an angel who is ordered to write four things. He is ordered to write down his (i.e. the new creature's) deeds, his livelihood, his (date of) death, and whether he will be blessed or wretched."

What the hadith says

At the fourth month of pregnancy, Allah sends an angel who writes four things about the fetus: all deeds they will perform, lifetime sustenance, exact date of death, and whether they will end in paradise or hell. The hadith extends this: a person apparently heading toward paradise can be redirected to hell because of what was pre-written.

Why this is a problem

  1. Paradise and hell are determined before a person takes their first breath. Their entire lifetime of deeds is also pre-written. Yet they will be held morally responsible and judged for those same deeds — creating the classic problem of punishing people for executing a script they did not write.
  2. The last-moment reversal makes free will theatrical. A person may spend a lifetime apparently heading toward paradise and then have their behavior reversed by the pre-written script at the final moment. The person's apparent choice was not real; the writing was.

Why it fails

The hadith's language is about writing (kataba), not foreknowing. The explicit extension — a man is redirected to hell "because of what has been written for him" after a lifetime near paradise — is causal, not descriptive. Compatibilism works as philosophical rescue, but the hadith describes a life-script written before birth, with the person acting it out and then being judged for a script they did not write.

Abraham told three lies — and refuses to intercede for humanity because of them Strange / Obscure Logical Inconsistency Strong Bukhari 3219
"Allah's Apostle said, 'Abraham did not tell a lie except on three occasions. Twice for the Sake of Allah when he said, "I am sick," and he said, "(I have not done this but) the big idol has done it." The (third was) that while Abraham and Sarah were going on a journey... Abraham said [about Sarah], "She is my sister."'"

What the hadith says

Muhammad identifies three specific lies Abraham told. In the Day-of-Judgment intercession hadith (Bukhari 3223), Abraham refuses to intercede for humanity, citing these very lies as his disqualification.

Why this is a problem

  1. Prophetic infallibility fails on its own evidence. Islamic doctrine holds prophets are ma'sum — protected from major sin. Muhammad explicitly calls these lies, and the intercession narrative requires them to be real moral disqualifications. The tradition cannot hold both prophetic sinlessness and Muhammad's own characterization of Abraham's conduct as lying.
  2. The Sarah lie is morally problematic even by ancient standards. Abraham protected himself by presenting his wife to a foreign ruler as his sister — allowing her to be taken by the tyrant while he survived. Whatever "lie for Allah's sake" means, this one benefits Abraham personally at a direct cost to his wife.

Why it fails

Muhammad's own word in the hadith is "he did not tell a lie except on three occasions" — the word is kadhib, the standard Arabic term for lying. The intercession narrative depends on Abraham having a real reason to feel disqualified before Allah. Apologetic reclassification drains the narrative of its logical structure while leaving the specific word Muhammad used unchanged.

Muhammad took a month to receive revelation about his wife's innocence — and considered divorcing her Prophetic Character Women Strong Bukhari 4551
"He had stayed a month without receiving any Divine Inspiration concerning my case. Allah's Apostle recited the Tashahhud after he had sat down, and then said, 'Thereafter, O Aisha! I have been informed such-and-such a thing about you; and if you are innocent, Allah will reveal your innocence, and if you have committed a sin, then ask for Allah's forgiveness...'"

What the hadith says

After rumors of Aisha's adultery spread, Muhammad did not defend her. For approximately a month he received no revelation on the matter, consulted companions about whether to divorce her, while Aisha wept throughout. Eventually Surah 24 arrived declaring her innocent.

Why this is a problem

  1. A prophet with access to divine knowledge could not establish his own wife's innocence. A month of uncertainty about something directly affecting his household is incompatible with the claim of reliable access to divine information.
  2. The revelation that arrived conveniently served multiple interests. It vindicated Aisha, established a legal standard (four witnesses) making future accusations nearly impossible to prove, and punished those who had spread the rumor. Aisha herself later observed that Muhammad's Lord "hastens in fulfilling your wishes" — the pattern of convenient revelation was visible to those closest to him.

Why it fails

If Muhammad had reliable access to divine knowledge, a month of silence on his wife's innocence suggests that access was not continuous or reliable. Aisha's own sardonic observation about convenient revelation — preserved in Bukhari — reflects an insider recognition that the pattern of timely divine authorization was visible to those in the household. The tradition preserves both the inconvenience and the convenient timing without resolving the tension.

After the conquest of Mecca, Muhammad ordered enemies killed even while clinging to the Ka'ba Prophetic Character Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Bukhari 1778
"The Prophet entered Mecca in the year of the Conquest wearing an Arabian helmet on his head; and when the Prophet took it off, a person came and said, 'Ibn Khatal is clinging to the curtains of the Ka'ba.' The Prophet said, 'Kill him.'"

What the hadith says

When Muhammad conquered Mecca in 630 CE, several specific individuals were marked for death. Ibn Khatal sought sanctuary by clinging to the Ka'ba — traditionally the most sacred Arabian space where violence was forbidden. Muhammad ordered him killed anyway. Beyond Ibn Khatal, the conquest period included the targeted assassination of several critics: poets and former Muslims who had mocked Muhammad.

Why this is a problem

The pattern across the conquest period — Ibn Khatal (apostate), Asma bint Marwan (poetess who criticized Muhammad), Abu Afak (elderly poet), An-Nadr bin al-Harith (literary critic) — shows systematic targeting of critics. General amnesty was extended to armed enemies; targeted executions were reserved for Muhammad's personal critics. This is the prophetic precedent for the principle that insulting Muhammad warrants death, a principle active in modern blasphemy law and fatwa practice.

Why it fails

General amnesty with a list of named exceptions communicates exactly what the exceptions signal: certain people — those who had criticized Muhammad personally — were beyond the scope of mercy that extended to armed enemies. The criterion is not danger or treason; it is opposition to Muhammad specifically. That criterion became the operating principle of Islamic blasphemy jurisprudence, and modern defenders of blasphemy killings are applying it, not distorting it.

The Quran was collected from "parchments, scapula, leaf-stalks, and from memories" after the Prophet's death Logical Inconsistency Strong Bukhari 4473
"So I started locating Quranic material and collecting it from parchments, scapula, leaf-stalks of date palms and from the memories of men (who knew it by heart). I found with Khuzaima two Verses of Surat-at-Tauba which I had not found with anybody else..."

What the hadith says

After Muhammad's death, following the Battle of Yamama where many Quran-memorizers were killed, Abu Bakr ordered Zaid bin Thabit to compile the Quran. Zaid gathered it from scattered fragments — parchments, animal shoulder blades, palm-leaf stalks, and memorizers' recitations. The last two verses of Surah 9 were found with only one person: Khuzaima.

Why this is a problem

  1. The Quran was never compiled during Muhammad's lifetime. It existed as fragments plus personal memorizations. Umar's concern — the impetus for the compilation — was explicitly that parts might be permanently lost as memorizers died.
  2. Some verses were found with only one person. If two verses were uniquely held by Khuzaima, the question arises: what about verses with no surviving witness at all? The hadith raises that possibility without answering it.

Why it fails

The "divine means through human instruments" framing cannot explain why Allah's preservation plan required Umar's fear of loss to initiate the project, or why the final text includes verses found with only one written witness. If Allah guaranteed preservation, the fragility of the process — and the explicit acknowledgment that preservation could have failed — is incompatible with a guarantee that operated at the text level rather than through human anxiety about loss.

"Even if Fatima had stolen, I would cut off her hand" — no exceptions to amputation for theft Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Bukhari 3329
"The Prophet said, 'The nations before you were destroyed because if a noble person committed theft, they used to leave him, but if a weak person amongst them committed theft, they used to inflict the legal punishment on him. By Allah, if Fatima, the daughter of Muhammad committed theft, I would cut off her hand.'"

What the hadith says

Muhammad enforced the Quranic amputation penalty for theft (Q 5:38) universally, citing equal application regardless of social status. He used his own daughter Fatima as the limiting case: even she would have her hand cut off.

Why this is a problem

The punishment is permanent and disproportionate. Classical Islamic law prescribes cutting the right hand for the first qualifying theft, the left foot for the second, with further amputations for subsequent offenses. Theft of property can be compensated through restitution — the harm can be undone. Amputation cannot. The punishment permanently disables a person, typically making the thief — often economically desperate — destitute. Saudi Arabia, Iran, Yemen, parts of Nigeria, Sudan, Somalia, and Afghanistan under the Taliban still apply hand amputation for theft as designed.

Why it fails

"Rarely applied" does not address the ethics of the punishment itself — it addresses the frequency. Where the conditions are met, the punishment is amputating a hand. Permanently disabling a person for property crime fails proportionality regardless of how narrowly the threshold is defined. Modern applications in Saudi Arabia and Taliban Afghanistan demonstrate the punishment is not theoretical.

Uthman burned all Quran manuscripts except his standardized version Logical Inconsistency Strong Bukhari 4780
"Uthman sent to every Muslim province one copy of what they had copied, and ordered that all the other Quranic materials, whether written in fragmentary manuscripts or whole copies, be burnt."

What the hadith says

The third caliph Uthman convened a committee to produce a single standardized Quran, then ordered all other Quranic manuscripts and versions burned throughout the Muslim world.

Why this is a problem

  1. Multiple Quran versions existed before Uthman. If the text were perfectly preserved from revelation, standardization would have been unnecessary. Significant variations in verse order, wording, and readings existed across different companions' codices.
  2. Ibn Mas'ud refused to surrender his copy. One of Muhammad's closest companions and acknowledged Quran experts refused to burn his version. His codex differed from Uthman's in verse order and some content. His refusal is preserved in Islamic historical records.

Why it fails

If Allah guaranteed preservation (Q 15:9), human burning of variants would be unnecessary for preservation — and sufficient to destroy it if the variants were genuine revelation. Ibn Mas'ud's refusal establishes that significant textual differences existed, not just pronunciation variations. The Sana'a palimpsest, discovered in 1972, shows a Quranic text under another with substantive differences from the Uthmanic version — physical evidence that editorial work occurred. Textual uniformity was enforced by fire, not secured by divine providence.

The Quran calls itself "clear" — yet required committee compilation and burning of variants Contradiction Logical Inconsistency Strong Quran 12:1
"These are the verses of the clear Book." (Quran 12:1) — vs. — Uthman ordered all other Quranic materials be burnt. (Bukhari)

What the texts say

The Quran repeatedly claims to be a clear, perfectly-preserved, divinely-authoritative book. The hadith tradition records how it actually came to exist in its current form: post-mortem committee compilation, recovery of some verses from single sources, burning of competing codices.

Why this is a problem

The two narratives fit poorly together. If the Quran is divinely clear, why did it need a committee after Muhammad's death to assemble? If divinely preserved, why did Uthman need to burn alternatives? If uniquely readable, why were there "seven ahruf" (multiple readings)? If comprehensive, why did Zaid gather fragments from palm-leaves and human memory? The hadith tradition is historically honest about these challenges — it records the compilation, the variants, and the burning. What it does not do is reconcile these records with the Quranic claim of self-preservation.

Why it fails

A book "guarded" by Allah (Q 15:9) does not require a caliph to burn competing versions to ensure its integrity. If Allah's guardianship was the mechanism, human burning was unnecessary. If human burning was necessary, divine guardianship was not sufficient. The tradition cannot hold both simultaneously without reducing Q 15:9's promise to "Allah guaranteed that humans would eventually compile it correctly" — which is a very different claim from the one the verse makes.
